coredata.dc:title: A smartphone-integrated optical sensing platform based on Lycium ruthenicum derived carbon dots for real-time detection of Ag+
coredata.dc:creator: Tang, Siyuan
Chen, Da
Guo, Guoqiang
Li, Xiameng
Wang, Changxing
Li, Tingting
Wang, Gang
coredata.dc:description: Growing global environmental pollution problems challenge the need for converting biomass into an advantageous product. In this paper, Lycium ruthenicum is successfully turned into beneficial green emissive (527 nm) fluorescent nitrogen doping carbon dots (N-CDs) via the hydrothermal treatment for the first time. The horizontal and vertical dimensions of N-CDs are demonstrated to be about 4.5 and 0.73 nm, respectively. The N-CDs possess an extremely stable green fluorescence and quantum yield up to 21.8%. Meaningfully, N-CDs exhibit a good linear relationship with Ag+ in the range of 0.7–36 μM, and its detection limit is determined to be 59 nM. The practicability of the fluorescent probe is further validated in lake water and the satisfactory spiked recoveries of Ag+ ranges from 98.99% to 104.19%. Besides, based on the sensitive and selective photoluminescence quenching properties, a smartphone-based laboratory device and RGB analysis software are used to directly capture and analyze fluorescence images with a sensitive detection limit of 83 nM for Ag+. This novel sensor based on N-CDs and smartphone provides a reliable way for on-site monitoring of Ag+ and expands application prospect in the field of environmental pollution detection.

head.ce:title: A smartphone-integrated optical sensing platform based on Lycium ruthenicum derived carbon dots for real-time detection of Ag+

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering, School of Physical Science and Technology, Ningbo University, Ningbo 315211, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering
School of Physical Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering, School of Physical Science and Technology, Ningbo University, Ningbo 315211, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Growing global environmental pollution problems challenge the need for converting biomass into an advantageous product. In this paper, Lycium ruthenicum is successfully turned into beneficial green emissive (527 nm) fluorescent nitrogen doping carbon dots (N-CDs) via the hydrothermal treatment for the first time. The horizontal and vertical dimensions of N-CDs are demonstrated to be about 4.5 and 0.73 nm, respectively. The N-CDs possess an extremely stable green fluorescence and quantum yield up to 21.8%. Meaningfully, N-CDs exhibit a good linear relationship with Ag+ in the range of 0.7–36 μM, and its detection limit is determined to be 59 nM. The practicability of the fluorescent probe is further validated in lake water and the satisfactory spiked recoveries of Ag+ ranges from 98.99% to 104.19%. Besides, based on the sensitive and selective photoluminescence quenching properties, a smartphone-based laboratory device and RGB analysis software are used to directly capture and analyze fluorescence images with a sensitive detection limit of 83 nM for Ag+. This novel sensor based on N-CDs and smartphone provides a reliable way for on-site monitoring of Ag+ and expands application prospect in the field of environmental pollution detection.

ce:section.ce:para: As a new kind of zero-dimensional nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) have captured immense interest of researchers on account of their excellent optical, thermal and electronic properties (Lesani et al., 2020; Yue et al., 2020; Li et al., 2020; Shen et al., 2020). Compared with traditional organic dyes that are expensive and contain cytotoxic heavy metal constituents, CDs show distinguished properties such as promising photoluminescence, superior biocompatibility, low toxicity and inexpensive cost (Wang et al., 2020a; Wei et al., 2020; Yuan et al., 2018). Considering these excellent material properties, CDs have been extensively applied in light emitting diode (Miao et al., 2018), solar battery (Gan et al., 2019), information encryption (Shen et al., 2019), fluorescence ink (Wang et al., 2020b), biological sensing (Song et al., 2017), bioimaging (Xu et al., 2020) and drug delivery (Sun et al., 2016). At present, CDs are prepared generally by “top-down” or “bottom-up” methods (Wang et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2015). The “top-down” methods mainly focus on the cutting and exfoliation from various sp 2 carbon materials (such as carbon fibers, carbon nanotube, C60, graphite and graphene oxides) by solvothermal, chemical and electrochemical processes (Kim et al., 2013). In contrast, “bottom-up” methods mainly involve assembling small molecules into larger CDs including the process of dehydration, polymerization, aromatization and carbonization by hydrothermal or microwave method (Zhu et al., 2017). The conjugation of small carbon molecules including glucose, citric acid and polyphenols by using the bottom-up strategy results in the well-controlled CDs with tunable size, shape and photoluminescence (PL) property (Tian et al., 2017). But in recent years, a large number of expensive and toxic chemicals are used to prepare CDs, which causing not only economic waste but also serious environmental pollution. To realize the need for economical and green materials, some carbon-containing natural resources, such as coal (Ye et al., 2013), papaya (Wang et al., 2016), orange juice (Sahu et al., 2012), passiflora edulia sims (Wang et al., 2020b), lecithin (Wang et al., 2019), durian (Wang et al., 2018) and konjac flour (Teng et al., 2014) are used as the precursors to synthesize CDs by hydrothermal treatment. For example, Li et al. (2017) prepared nitrogen doped carbon dots from ginkgo fruits with a quantum yield (QY) of 3.33% by one-step hydrothermal methods and reported the application in cell imaging. Atchudan et al. (2017) utilized Chionanthus retusus fruit to synthesize nitrogen-doped carbon dots and the QY is around 9%, which was used as biological probes to detect Fe3+. However, these CDs are not enable to achieve high QY values (usually<10%) and excellent luminescence stability, thus hindering its large-scale and bioimaging applications. Therefore, it is urgent to find eco-friendly natural resources to prepare a high QY, excellent biocompatibility and stable photoluminescence CDs.

As a novel luminescent nanomaterial, CDs have been widely used in the detection of metal pollutants in environmental water system. Among these metals, silver ion, an extremely lively positively charged cation, is popularly utilized in industrial production and antibacterial sterilization, leading to a large amount of environmental water pollution, which indirectly threatens human health (Lu et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2021). In addition, Ag+ belongs to the trace elements in the human tissue and excessive Ag+ in drinking water will damage the human cells (Feng et al., 2019). Conventional instruments for Ag+ analysis, such as chromatography, mass spectrometry and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), have achieved satisfactory accuracy and resolution (Lu et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2021; Feng et al., 2019; An et al., 2021). Although these methods being featured with excellent ability, there are still remain difficulties in on-site monitoring field due to the operational complexity, time-consuming procedure and requirement. In recent years, a lot of efforts have been made to establish reliable, portable and real-time Ag+ sensing technology (Lu et al., 2021; Chen et al., 2019; Xiao et al., 2019). For example, Chen et al. (2019) proposed a home-made portable device based on a 32 bit embed Micro Control Unit and CdTe quantum dots system for Ag+ detection. Xiao et al. (2019) designed a novel 3D origami microfluidic paper-based analytical devices as an Ag+ sensor. However, these methods can only perform qualitative analysis, not real-time quantitative detection. With the progress of high sensitivity and accurate on-site monitoring instruments, the detection platform equipped with a portable device has attracted much attentions due to its portability and simplicity (Yang et al., 2021). Among these portable devices, colorimetric image analysis method is taken into consideration because of their high efficiency and low price (Ehtesabi et al., 2021). In this method, the images are divided into their main red, green and blue color components (RGB). Compared to conventional qualitative analysis methods, the portable devices-based digital image colorimetry allows convenient real-time field analysis quantitatively. Therefore, it is significant to develop on-site and rapid detection equipment integrating image colorimetry and portable devices.

In order to prepare environmentally sustainable, eco-friendly CDs and provide a simple, convenient and rapid on-site quantitative detection method for metal ions, we demonstrate the use of Lycium ruthenicum as a new carbon source for the green synthesis of water-soluble and high fluorescent N-CDs and design a portable device to on-site quantitative detection of Ag+ (Scheme 1 ). Lycium ruthenicum is the natural fruit with the highest anthocyanin content, and also contains a variety of amino acids, e.g. aspartic acid, glutamic acid, alanine and proline, which provides abundant sources of carbon and nitrogen for the preparation of N-CDs. In this contribution, the as-synthesized N-CDs emit strong green fluorescence under 365 nm UV light with a high quantum yield of 21.8%. Meanwhile, N-CDs can serve as a fully effective fluorescent probe for label-free, sensitive and selective detection of Ag+ with a linear range from 0.7–36 μM with a detection limit of 59 nM. Subsequently, an integrated optical sensing platform based on N-CDs and smartphone is developed, which is used for real-time detection of Ag+ via a RGB values analysis application. Therefore, this work not only provides a new approach for synthesis of eco-friendly N-CDs, but also develops a smartphone-based handheld platform for the on-site monitoring Ag+ ions in food safety monitoring and environmental pollution analysis.

The Lycium ruthenicum is bought from the local supermarket near Ningbo University (Ningbo, China). FeCl3 (≥99.99%, CAS: 7705-08-0), CuSO4·5H2O (99.99%, CAS: 7758-99-8), CaCl2·2H2O (99.99%, CAS: 10035-04-8), CoCl2 (99.7%, CAS: 7646-79-9), ZnCl2 (99.999%, CAS: 7646-85-7), AgNO3 (99.99%, CAS: 7761-88-8), CdCl2 (99.99%, CAS: 10108-64-2), MgSO4 (99.99%, CAS: 7487-88-9), NaCl (99.99%, CAS: 7647-14-5), MnCl2·4H2O (99.99%, CAS: 13446-34-9), FeCl2·4H2O (99.95%, CAS: 13478-10-9), NiCl2·6H2O (99.9%, CAS: 7791-20-0), PbCl2 (99.99%, CAS: 7758-95-4), K2CO3 (99.99%, CAS: 584-08-7), AlCl3 (99.99%, CAS: 7446-70-0), CrCl3 (99.99%, CAS: 10025-73-7), HgCl2 (≥99.5%, CAS: 7487-94-7) and all anionic solutions are purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China) and all reagents are used directly without further purification. Ultrapure water used throughout all experiments is purified via a Millipore system with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ·cm.

Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectra are obtained with UV-5800 spectrophotometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra are performed on an Axis Ultra DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer and a Thermo Scientific Nicolet 6700 FT-IR spectrometer. The morphology and the particle size of N-CDs are carried out on JEM-2100 transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Atomic Force Microscope (Cypher S, Asylum research) is used to determine the height profiles of N-CDs. Fluorescence spectroscopy and lifetime experiments are measured using Hitachi F-7000 instrument (Hitachi, Japan) at room temperature. Raman spectra are collected on a HORIBA Jobin Yvon HR800 spectrometer with the excitation wavelength of 532 nm.

N-CDs are prepared via a hydrothermal method using Lycium ruthenicum as carbon and nitrogen precursors. In a typical synthesis, 3.0 g Lycium ruthenicum flesh is crushed and stirred with 20.0 mL deionized water for 30 min. The obtained aqueous dispersion is transferred into a 50.0 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 24 h until a dark brownish solution is formed. After that, the autoclave is cooled down to room temperature naturally. The obtained solution is centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 15 min, and then filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane to remove large particles. Finally, the obtained N-CDs solution is dialysed by a dialysis bag (MWCO: 100–500 Da) for 48 h and stored at below 4 °C for further uses.

Rhodamine 6G (QY = 95% in ethanol at 420 nm) is selected as the standard sample. The quantum yields of N-CDs are calculated according to the following Equation (Tang et al., 2022),
(1)
φ =
φ R
×
I
I R

A R
A
×
η 2
η R 2
where φ is the quantum yield, I is the integrated emission intensity, η is the refractive index of the solvent, A is the optical density, and the subscript R refers to the reference standard with a known quantum yield.

The detection of Ag+ is performed at room temperature. In a typical assay, 200 μL of N-CDs dispersion (5 mg/mL) is added into a series of solution containing various concentrations of Ag+. The fluorescence emission spectra are recorded after reaction for 5 min at room temperature. The sensitivity and selectivity measurements are conducted in triplicate. The selectivity of Ag+ sensing is confirmed by adding other common ions stock solutions (including Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Al3+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Cl−, Br−, SO4 2−, NO3 − and S2− ions) instead of Ag+ in a similar way.

For smartphone-based quantitative analysis Ag+, 2 mL of various concentrations of Ag+ and 2 mL of N-CDs solution are mixed into the cuvette. After equilibration for 3 min, the cuvette is inserted into the 3D-printed smartphone Holder to obtain the fluorescence image under the excitation of 365 nm UV light. Then, the displayed photo image is directly analyzed using an app installed on the smartphone. The sum RGB values using Equation,
(2)
Sum
RGB
values = Red + Green + Blue

Fibroblast cells grown on 18 × 18 mm2 glass coverslips were first cultured in Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM, Thermo Fisher Scientific) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS), 100 mg·mL−1 glutamine, 100 mg·mL−1 sodium pyruvate, penicillin (100 units·mL−1), and streptomycin (100 units·mL−1) at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere overnight followed by incubation with 300 μg·mL−1 N-CDs for 24 h. Finally, cell imaging was performed with Zeiss LSM 780 confocal lasers scanning fluorescence microscopy.

We characterized the prepared N-CDs by TEM and AFM to investigate their structures. A typical TEM image of obtained N-CDs is shown in Fig. 1(a), and the observed N-CDs are mainly spherical dots-shape morphology with analogous size. At a higher resolution TEM in Fig. 1(b), a randomly selected N-CDs exhibit honeycomb structure, indicating the conjugated sp2-domains structure of carbon core with high graphitization degree. The lattice parameter of 0.24 nm corresponds the (100) in-plane lattice of graphene (Fig. S1) (Yang et al., 2019). A hexagonal symmetry pattern in the fast Fourier transform (FFT) analysis in the inset of Fig. 1(b) reveals the highly graphitized core structure of N-CDs and confirms the result of high resolution TEM. The N-CDs have a size distribution from 3.0 to 6.5 nm with an average diameter of 4.5 nm (Fig. 1(c)). AFM is carried out to characterize the morphological height of N-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1(d), the distinguishable N-CDs exhibit uniform distribution and the statistical data of thickness according to the two dotted lines in Fig. 1(d) is presented in Fig. 1(e–f). The average thickness of 0.73 nm indicates few graphite layers of carbon core (Fig. 1(g)). The XRD pattern of N-CDs (Fig. S2) suggests a broad diffraction peak at 21o (d = 0.42 nm) and can be ascribe to the (002) planes of graphitized carbon (Dervishi et al., 2019).

XPS and FT-IR are carried out to evaluate the chemical composition and structure of N-CDs. In the XPS spectrum of N-CDs (Fig. 2(a)), three peaks at about 285, 400 and 533 eV can describe to the C, N and O element, respectively. It can be inferred that the C and N element comes from Lycium ruthenicum and O comes from Lycium ruthenicum and water. The C 1s spectrum shown in Fig. 2(b) reveals four chemical states of C atoms: CC/CC, CN, CO and CO, corresponding to 284.0, 285.2, 286.9 and 289.9 eV, respectively (Lee et al., 2019; Dager et al., 2019; Qu et al., 2014). The N 1s spectrum (Fig. 2(c)) displays three peaks at 399.8, 400.2 and 402.1 eV, which are consistent with pyridinic N, amino N and pyrrolic N, respectively (Bian et al., 2019; Lu et al., 2019). In addition, the peaks at 530.9, 532.3 and 533.2 eV in O 1s spectrum (Fig. 2(d)) are ascribed to CO, CO and NO, respectively (Song et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019). The FT-IR spectrum in Fig. S3 indicates that the peaks at 1100 and 1380 cm−1 correspond to CO bond (Ghosh et al., 2019). Moreover, the peaks located at 1253 and 1630 cm−1 can be ascribed to CN and CO bonds (Zhang et al., 2019). Besides, the broad peak cover from 3180 to 3700 cm−1 be interpreted to OH and NH groups (Tong et al., 2020). Based on the above analysis, it can be inferred that the rich anthocyanins and amino acids in Lycium ruthenicum lead to the formation of rich nitrogen-containing and oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of N-CDs, which are conducive to further modification and exploitation.

The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra are utilized for investigating the optical properties of N-CDs. As shown in Fig. 3(a), the two absorption peaks located at 230 and 280 nm may be ascribed to π-π* of CC bond and n-π* of CO bond (Wang et al., 2020b). Besides, the broad and weak absorption band between 300 and 410 nm corresponds to the n-π* transition of N-related surface states or defect bonds (Chao et al., 2020). The optimal excitation and emission wavelength of N-CDs are located at 427 and 527 nm, respectively. Accordingly, the N-CDs solution under daylight and 365 nm ultraviolet light are shown in the inset of Fig. 3(a). A bright green fluorescence from N-CDs can be observed under UV light. In order to determine the color of fluorescence more closely, the Commission International d'Eclairage (CIE) chromaticity coordinate of (0.24, 0.68) is shown Fig. S4, indicating a pure green fluorescence of N-CDs under optimal excitation wavelength of 427 nm. Moreover, the PL wavelength red shifts from 515 to 574 nm with the increase of photoluminescence excitation (PLE) wavelength from 395 to 475 nm (Fig. 3(b)). The conspicuous excitation-dependent emission is caused by different emissive states (Feng et al., 2019). The 3D mapping of PL excitation and emission wavelength shown in Fig. 3(c) displays a large area, indicating the strong green fluorescence emission center of N-CDs. Furthermore, the QY of N-CDs is calculated to be 21.8%, which is higher than most doped CDs reported in the literatures (Table S1).

Excellent fluorescence stability endows the fluorescence sensors capability and ability. Therefore, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs with different conditions is studied. Fig. 3(d) shows the PL intensity of N-CDs under different pH values. The PL intensity firstly enhanced and reached at a highest value, and subsequently decreased by increasing pH value from 7 to 11. This change trend may be due to the protonation and deprotonation of surface group under alkaline and acidic environments (Long et al., 2020). The N-CDs exhibit tiny attenuation on PL intensity under irradiation of daylight for 8 days (Fig. 3(e)) and ultraviolet light for 16 h (Fig. S5), demonstrating the potent radiation resistance of N-CDs. Such phenomenon may be due to the stable chemical structure and surface functional group of N-CDs including hydroxyl, carbonyl and amino. Moreover, the highly concentrated NaCl can impose ignorable influences on the PL intensity even when the concentration up to 1 M (Fig. 3(f)). Above results indicate the high stability of N-CDs, providing a solid foundation for potential applications. In addition, due to the splendid PL performance of N-CDs, the potential in bio-imaging is also evaluated. First, the in-vitro cytotoxicity of N-CDs is assessed by monitoring the viability of fibroblast cells. As shown in Fig. S6(a), the cell alive is merely decreased by 5% when the concentration of N-CDs reaches to 300 μg/mL, indicating the distinguished cell compatibility of N-CDs. Fibroblast cell cultured with 300 μg/mL N-CDs aqueous solution is observed by a confocal microscope in Fig. S6(b). It can be clearly observed that cells emit obvious green fluorescence, demonstrating the N-CDs are a potential nanomaterial for fluorescent bio-imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ag+ sensing using N-CDs based on fluorescence spectroscopy
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence responses of N-CDs toward potential interferents are investigated. As shown in Fig. S7, the fluorescence intensity has little change with the addition of the interfering substances (blue column). Only the addition of Ag+ causes the major decrease of the fluorescence intensity, which indicates the presence of the interfering substances has no significant effect on the determination of Ag+ (yellow column). These results confirm the high selectivity of N-CDs toward Ag+. Enlightened by the ranking anti-interference ability of N-CDs, a rapid and sensitive fluorescent probe is contrived using N-CDs by fluorescence spectroscopy. The sensing property of N-CDs is evaluated by the detection of Ag+ under the excitation of 427 nm. Fig. S8 exhibits the (F0-F)/F0 of N-CDs under different pH values from 3 to 11 (F0 and F represent the PL intensity of N-CDs before and after the addition of Ag+, respectively.). It can be observed the least influence on fluorescence intensity occurs under the pH = 7, indicating the optimal detection pH value is 7. Fig. S9 displays the relationship between (F0-F)/F0 with reaction time, which indicates that the quenching reaction reached a complete state in the 3 min after the addition of Ag+ solution.

Based on the optimal reaction pH and time explored above, the performance of N-CDs for detecting Ag+ by fluorescence spectroscopy is further assessed. Fig. 4(a) shows the PL spectrum of N-CDs solution with the addition of different concentrations of Ag+ from 0 to 120 μM. The observed PL intensity decreases as the concentration of Ag+ increases. Quantitative data of (F0-F)/F0 and concentration of Ag+ are shown in Fig. 4(b) and an obvious linear relationship (R2 = 0.9953) is obtained in the range of 0.7 to 36 μM with the limit of detection of 59 nM, which is lower than most limits in reported literature (Tabaraki and Nateghi, 2016; Arumugam and Kim, 2018; Cayuela et al., 2016). Subsequently, the real application of N-CDs for the assay of Ag+ is recorded. Table S2 shows the recovery rates for the detection of Ag+ in lake water. This value varies between 98.99% and 104.19% and the relative standard deviation (RSD) is less than 2.07.

To investigate the mechanism of Ag+ detection of the N-CDs, we collect the UV–vis absorbance of N-CDs (curve i), Ag+ (curve ii), N-CDs@Ag+ compound aqueous solution (curve iii) and theoretical curve of curve i + curve ii (curve iv) in Fig. 4(c). Compared with the theoretical curve (curve iv), there is no obvious peak shift in the curve iii can be observed, which reveals that no complex is formed between N-CDs and Ag+ (Zhou et al., 2017). Moreover, the PLE and PL spectra of N-CDs exist no overlapping with the UV–vis absorbance of Ag+ aqueous solution, as shown in Fig. S10. This result suggests that the Ag+ induced PL suppression cannot be interpreted as the result of inner filter effect (IFE) or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) (Zhou et al., 2017; Zu et al., 2017). Interestingly, compared with curve i and curve iii, there is no change on the types of light absorption peak after the addition of Ag+, but the absorbance intensity of N-CDs at 200–250 nm is obviously enhanced, which may be ascribed to the facilitated non-radiative recombination of the excitons by Ag+ (Ren et al., 2017). Meanwhile, 10 μM of other metal ions (e.g. Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Mg2+) can only cause tiny change to the absorbance of N-CDs (Fig. S11). Thence, it is inferred that Ag+ ions can be seen as electron acceptor and promote the electron transfer from N-CDs to Ag+, leading to the PL quenching of N-CDs. To prove the reliability of the explanation, the zeta potentials of N-CDs with different concentrations of Ag+ are performed in Fig. 4(d). It is worth noting that the Zeta potential of N-CDs decreases gradually from −50 to −33.6 mV with the increase of Ag+ concentration, which indicates that Ag+ can cause electron-transfer from the N-CDs to the Ag+ ions (Zu et al., 2017; Ren et al., 2017). Fig. 4(e) shows a possible electron transfer process between N-CDs and Ag+. When the distance between the N-CDs and Ag+ is close enough, electrons in the excited state of N-CDs will transfer to the unfilled orbit of Ag+, which results in the nonradiative electron/hole recombination and fluorescence quenching rapidly of N-CDs.

Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity based on Stern-Volmer plot is also conducted to gain insight into the fluorescence quenching mechanism of N-CDs by Ag+ (Fig. S12). The PL quenching is analyzed using the Stern-Volmer Equation at 298 K (Song et al., 2014),
(3)
F 0
/ F − 1 =
K q
τ 0
Q
=
K sv
Q
where F0 and F represent the PL intensities in the absence and presence of Ag+, respectively, Ksv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, [Q] is the concentration of the quencher, Kq is the quenching rate constant, and τ0 is the fluorescence lifetime of the N-CDs. As presented in Fig. S12, (F0/F-1) shows a linear correlation with the concentration of Ag+ between 0 and 12 μM (F0/F-1 = 0.02804[Ag+] − 0.01213), suggesting that the Stern-Volmer quenching constant Ksv = 0.02804 μM−1. Meanwhile, τ0 is calculated as 5.61 ns (Fig. 4(f)) by time-correlated single photon counting, and then Kq is calculated to be 4.99 × 1012 M−1·s−1. This value is unusually high, indicating that it is static quenching rather than dynamic quenching because the rate constant for dynamic quenching is generally considered to be less than 1.0 × 1010 M−1·s−1 (Yue et al., 2019; Hu et al., 2020). Subsequently, the PL decay curves of N-CDs and N-CDs@Ag+ compound aqueous solution are calculated as 5.61 and 5.58 ns (Fig. 4(f)), respectively. The negligible change of average PL lifetime indicates no significant excited-state interaction between N-CDs and Ag+, suggesting the PL quenching is caused by static quenching effect (SQE) (Zhou et al., 2017; Ren et al., 2017). Over all, the detection mechanism of Ag+ based on N-CDs is attributable to synergetic effect of electron transfer and SQE.

At present, the requirements of quantitative analysis of analyte can be satisfied by the standard test instrument. Nonetheless, on account of the complexity of operation and experimental instrument limitation, the portable on-site monitoring still facing challenges (Zhang et al., 2021; Feng et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2021). Therefore, it is of great sense to develop a portable and integrated device for ions quantitative analysis. Herein, we adopt N-CDs to construct the handheld smartphone fluorescence sensing device, which equipped with a sample loading platform and a UV light source. This strategy can effectively decrease the error from environmental and operation factor, and it can solve the problems of the image acquisition and data analysis. As shown in Figs. 5, S13 and Video S1, the integrated equipment design (sample optical imaging, equipment structure and image-processing) based on iPhone 12 smartphone is presented. The black paint is applied to the outside of the device to reduce the penetration of the ambient light source. During the imaging process, mini UV light generator (100 mW) with a wavelength of 365 nm serve as a light source of excitation powered by a common 1.5 V AA size battery and the fluorescent images are collected by the smartphone camera. As a result, the portable device based on high-performance N-CDs and smartphone have the potential of on-site quantitative sensing ions, and possess a high sensitivity and low background noise. Meanwhile, it is satisfactory that the cost of handheld device just $5, which greatly expands the possibility of commercialization. Considering the data processing capacity of the smartphone, we adopt an application called “ColorDesk” for data processing of images. The software can control the smartphone to collect photo images and obtain the corresponding RGB values of these photographs.

At present, the requirements of quantitative analysis of analyte can be satisfied by the standard test instrument. Nonetheless, on account of the complexity of operation and experimental instrument limitation, the portable on-site monitoring still facing challenges (Zhang et al., 2021; Feng et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2021). Therefore, it is of great sense to develop a portable and integrated device for ions quantitative analysis. Herein, we adopt N-CDs to construct the handheld smartphone fluorescence sensing device, which equipped with a sample loading platform and a UV light source. This strategy can effectively decrease the error from environmental and operation factor, and it can solve the problems of the image acquisition and data analysis. As shown in Figs. 5, S13 and Video S1, the integrated equipment design (sample optical imaging, equipment structure and image-processing) based on iPhone 12 smartphone is presented. The black paint is applied to the outside of the device to reduce the penetration of the ambient light source. During the imaging process, mini UV light generator (100 mW) with a wavelength of 365 nm serve as a light source of excitation powered by a common 1.5 V AA size battery and the fluorescent images are collected by the smartphone camera. As a result, the portable device based on high-performance N-CDs and smartphone have the potential of on-site quantitative sensing ions, and possess a high sensitivity and low background noise. Meanwhile, it is satisfactory that the cost of handheld device just $5, which greatly expands the possibility of commercialization. Considering the data processing capacity of the smartphone, we adopt an application called “ColorDesk” for data processing of images. The software can control the smartphone to collect photo images and obtain the corresponding RGB values of these photographs.

The practicability and accuracy of portable device is of significance in on-site quantitative detection. Therefore, the device is used for the actual analysis of the sample and evaluation of results. As shown in Fig. 6(a), the photo images of N-CDs solutions with different Ag+ concentrations can be clearly captured by a smartphone camera under 365 nm UV light irradiation. Blind repetitions of the measurement on the fluorescent probe response to N-CDs + Ag+ (3 μM) solution reveal a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 0.21% (n = 6 times), which displays that this analytical method yields reproducible results (Fig. 6(b)). Subsequently, the RGB values of the digital images are converted to corresponding color scale card and evaluated with app software (Fig. 6(c) and Video S2). The relationship between the sum of RGB values and the Ag+ concentrations is depicted in Fig. 6(d). From the plot, a linear range of 0–20 μM is obtained and the limit of detection is calculated to be 83 nM (3σ/slope). Although the detection limit is slightly higher than that of fluorescence analysis based on N-CDs, the portable handheld device has unique comprehensive advantages in detection limit and inspection time compared with other methods reported in literature (Table S3). Therefore, these results indicate that the handheld device developed in this work provides a strategy for portable on-site quantitative monitoring of Ag+.

The practicability and accuracy of portable device is of significance in on-site quantitative detection. Therefore, the device is used for the actual analysis of the sample and evaluation of results. As shown in Fig. 6(a), the photo images of N-CDs solutions with different Ag+ concentrations can be clearly captured by a smartphone camera under 365 nm UV light irradiation. Blind repetitions of the measurement on the fluorescent probe response to N-CDs + Ag+ (3 μM) solution reveal a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 0.21% (n = 6 times), which displays that this analytical method yields reproducible results (Fig. 6(b)). Subsequently, the RGB values of the digital images are converted to corresponding color scale card and evaluated with app software (Fig. 6(c) and Video S2). The relationship between the sum of RGB values and the Ag+ concentrations is depicted in Fig. 6(d). From the plot, a linear range of 0–20 μM is obtained and the limit of detection is calculated to be 83 nM (3σ/slope). Although the detection limit is slightly higher than that of fluorescence analysis based on N-CDs, the portable handheld device has unique comprehensive advantages in detection limit and inspection time compared with other methods reported in literature (Table S3). Therefore, these results indicate that the handheld device developed in this work provides a strategy for portable on-site quantitative monitoring of Ag+.

In general, we have reported a simple, green, and low-cost synthetic strategy to prepare green emissive N-CDs derived from Lycium ruthenicum. The as-prepared N-CDs possess a high QY of 21.8%, stable photoluminescence and excellent biocompatibility. Moreover, N-CDs show the advantages on high sensitivity and good selectivity in the detection of Ag+. Besides, a handheld smartphone fluorescence sensing device based on N-CDs fluorescence system is designed. By analyzing the RGB values, the sensing system has a low detection limit of 83 nM and achieves the rapid, convenient and on-site quantitative detection of Ag+ in real sample. Thus, it is believed that portable devices based on N-CDs fluorescent probes will provide promising real-time detection application potential in food safety monitoring and environmental pollution analysis in the future.

The following are the supplementary data related to this article.
Lattice pattern of N-CDs; XRD pattern of N-CDs; FT-IR spectrum of N-CDs; CIE coordinate of N-CDs; PL intensity of N-CDs under different irradiation time under UV light; fibroblast cells cultured with N-CDs observed by a confocal microscope under the excitation of 365 nm; effect of the different interfering substances on the detection of Ag+ by N-CDs; quenching effect with the pH values; quenching effect with the reaction time; UV-vis absorption spectrum of Ag+; UV-vis absorption spectrum of N-CDs with different ions; linear relationship between (F0-F)/F and Ag+ concentrations; actual device images of smartphone fluorometric sensing platform; table of QY of different CDs; table of detection of Ag+ in lake water; table comparing the sensing platforms for Ag+.
